items.title: Isabella Robot Documentation

items.title: Overview
items.text: This document explains which directories and files on the ARI robot are persistent across reboots, which should be backed up, and how to change non-persistent configurations.

items.title: Persistent Directories
items.text:
Dir location Description Make copy Justification /media/root-ro Main filesystem (read-only) N Unique data removed on reinstall /boot/efi Bootloader filesystem N Same for each install /etc/calibration PAL-specific calibrations and user settings Y Needed for robot operation, unique since delivery /home User home dir, includes .ros and calibration Y Apps use calibration data from here /var/log Log files N~ Usually not unique, too large to copy

Also back up /etc/fstab (contains boot mounting and settings).

items.title: Checking Mounts and Partitions
items.text:
Use the following commands to inspect disk partitions and mounts:

lsblk
mount
df

output as seen on robot: 

pal@ari-20c:~$ df
Filesystem     1K-blocks     Used Available Use% Mounted on
udev             7847008        0   7847008   0% /dev
tmpfs            1574044     1856   1572188   1% /run
/dev/nvme0n1p3 146658236 16825092 122310484  13% /media/root-ro
tmpfs-root       7870212    24892   7845320   1% /media/root-rw
overlayroot      7870212    24892   7845320   1% /
tmpfs            7870212     8192   7862020   1% /dev/shm
tmpfs               5120        4      5116   1% /run/lock
tmpfs            7870212        0   7870212   0% /sys/fs/cgroup
/dev/nvme0n1p2    122990     8810    114180   8% /boot/efi
/dev/nvme0n1p1     58085      189     53310   1% /etc/calibration
/dev/nvme0n1p5 266329820  9510416 243217412   4% /home
/dev/nvme0n1p6  76270372  6587712  65762584  10% /var/log
tmpfs            1574040        8   1574032   1% /run/user/1000

items.title: Changing Non-Persistent Configurations
items.text:
To edit configuration files (e.g., PID settings):

  1. go into admin mode: 

    su

  2. Remount filesystem as read-write and enter it: 

    sudo rw
chroot /ro

  3. Edit the file or install pkg (using vi if nano is not available): 
    sudo vi /opt/pal/gallium/share/ari_controller_configuration/config/pids_v2.yaml
  4. when done exit the filesystem: 
    exit
  5. Remount as read-only: 
    sudo ro
  6. then exit admin mode: 
    exit

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
• If unable to remount /ro as read-only, ensure no processes are using it.
  • Always back up important configuration files before making changes.

items.title: Intents
items.text: info. Description stock component No contains speculation No Location. Software (HRI/Interaction) related artical HRI Intents White Paper

items.title: Description
items.text: Intents are used in ARI's HRI system to interpret and respond to user commands and behaviors. The system is based on research and white papers from PAL Robotics.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Enables natural language understanding and interaction.
  • Used for task planning and execution.
  • Based on published research and best practices.

items.title: Intents not recognized
items.text:
• Check that the intent recognition node is running.
  • Review logs for errors in the HRI pipeline.

items.title: Isabella Robot Overview
items.text: Isabella Robot is an advanced mobile platform designed for social interaction, perception, and autonomous navigation.

items.title: Key Features
items.text:
• Mobile Base: Durable navigation and movement capabilities
  • Perception: Depth camera, LiDAR, and social perception systems
  • Voice Interaction: Respeaker microphone array for audio processing
  • Intelligent Core: LLM-based decision making and intent recognition
  • ROS Integration: Full Robot Operating System support for extensibility

items.title: Architecture Highlights
items.text:
• Modular software design using ROS nodes and services
  • Persistent filesystem for data and learning
  • Network communication via Docker containerization
  • Real-time transform (TF) system for coordinate management

items.title: Quick Start
items.text:
To get started with Isabella:

  1. Review the hardware setup
  2. Install required software dependencies
  3. Configure networking and Docker
  4. Run the test suite
  5. Explore the ROS nodes and services

items.title: Project Structure
items.text:
Isabella_Robot_Doc/
├── docs/
│   ├── hardware/
│   ├── software/
│   ├── architecture/
│   └── networking/
├── mkdocs.yml
└── zensical.toml

See the sidebar navigation for detailed documentation on each system.

items.title: Quick Start Guide
items.text: Get Isabella Robot up and running in minutes.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Ubuntu 20.04 or later
  • Python 3.8+
  • ROS Noetic or newer
  • Docker and Docker Compose
  • Git

items.title: 1. Clone the Repository
items.text:
git clone https://github.com/NM-TAFE/Isabella_Robot_Doc.git
cd Isabella_Robot_Doc

items.title: 2. Install Dependencies
items.text: pip install -r requirements.txt

items.title: 3. Build Documentation Locally
items.text:
mkdocs serve

Visit http://localhost:8000 to view the documentation.

items.title: 4. Build Static Site
items.text:
mkdocs build

The static site will be generated in the site/ directory.

items.title: Running Tests
items.text:
# Run the test suite
python -m pytest tests/

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Explore Hardware Documentation
  • Review Software Architecture
  • Configure Networking

items.title: Getting Help
items.text: Check the documentation index or visit the GitHub repository.

items.title: Running Tests
items.text: Isabella Robot includes comprehensive test suites for validation and verification.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
Ensure you have the following installed:

  • pytest
  • unittest (included with Python)
  • Docker (for integration tests)

items.title: Unit Tests
items.text:
Run all unit tests:

pytest tests/unit/

Run specific test file:

pytest tests/unit/test_localizer.py -v

items.title: Integration Tests
items.text:
Run integration tests (requires Docker):

pytest tests/integration/

items.title: Test Coverage
items.text:
Generate coverage report:

pytest --cov=src tests/

items.title: Test Structure
items.text:
tests/
├── unit/              # Unit tests for individual components
├── integration/       # Integration tests for multiple systems
└── fixtures/          # Test data and configurations

items.title: Continuous Integration
items.text: Tests are automatically run on every pull request via GitHub Actions.

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
If tests fail:

  1. Ensure all dependencies are installed: pip install -r requirements.txt
  2. Check Docker daemon is running: docker status
  3. Review test output for specific error messages
  4. Check the GitHub repository for known issues

items.title: Contributing Tests
items.text:
When contributing new features, please include:

  • Unit tests for new functions
  • Integration tests for new systems
  • Documentation of test procedures

items.title: Presentation Screen
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation No Location. Web interface related artical ARI Presentation Screen

items.title: Description
items.text: The ARI robot provides a web-based presentation screen for displaying information and interacting with users. This can be accessed via a browser on the local network.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Accessible at http://ari-20c:11011/webapps/pages/pal_touch_web_display/
  • Used for presentations, user interaction, and status display.

items.title: Screen not loading
items.text:
• Check network connection to ARI.
  • Ensure the web server is running on the robot.

items.title: Depth Camera
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation No Location. Torso Front (RGB-D RealSense D435i), Torso Back (Intel Realsense T265, stereo-fisheye) related artical Depth Camera - Computerphile

items.title: Description
items.text: The ARI robot uses two depth cameras: a RealSense D435i on the torso front and an Intel Realsense T265 (stereo-fisheye) on the torso back. These provide RGB-D and stereo depth data for navigation and perception.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• The D435i provides RGB-D data for front-facing perception.
  • The T265 provides stereo-fisheye data for rear perception and localization.
  • Both are standard components in the ARI platform.

items.title: Camera not detected
items.text:
• Check physical connections and USB ports.
  • Verify drivers are installed and ROS nodes are running.

items.title: Poor depth quality
items.text:
• Clean camera lenses.
  • Ensure adequate lighting and no obstructions.

items.title: Docking Station
items.text:
note: our unit did not come with docking stations and we do not currently have any

info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation No Location. See ARI documentation for physical location related artical aruco_ros GitHub

items.title: Description
items.text: The docking station is used for automatic charging and parking of the ARI robot. It uses visual markers (e.g., ArUco) for alignment and docking.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Provides a safe and reliable way for ARI to recharge autonomously.
  • Uses visual fiducials for precise alignment.
  • Integrated with ARI's navigation and power management systems.

items.title: Robot fails to dock
items.text:
• Check visual markers are clean and visible.
  • Ensure docking station is powered and unobstructed.
  • Verify ARI's navigation and docking nodes are running.

items.title: Lidar
items.text:
note: our unit doesn't have this part

info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation Yes Location. Base of robot (see ARI documentation) related artical DFRobot Lidar, RGB-D 3D ToF Sensor, YDLIDAR TG15

items.title: Description
items.text: The ARI robot uses a LIDAR sensor for navigation and mapping. The most common model is the YDLIDAR TG15, as referenced in the Autonomous Navigation Pack and configuration files.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• LIDAR is used for SLAM, obstacle avoidance, and navigation.
  • The YDLIDAR TG15 is the default, but other models may be supported.
  • Vision and laser-based self-localization and mapping (SLAM).
  • Available RViZ and web plugins for navigation.

!speculation: There are mentions of RGBD sensors and other LIDAR models, but YDLIDAR TG15 is most likely.

items.title: LIDAR not detected
items.text:
• Check power and data connections.
  • Verify correct model is set in ROS launch files.

items.title: Poor mapping or navigation
items.text:
• Ensure LIDAR is unobstructed and clean.
  • Check for correct calibration and configuration in ROS.

items.title: Respeaker Microphone Array
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation Yes Location. Head of robot (see ARI documentation) related artical Seeed Respeaker, Seeed Wiki, ODAS, VOSK

items.title: Description
items.text: The Respeaker USB Microphone Array provides far-field voice pickup and is used for speech recognition and sound source localization on the ARI robot.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Plug & Play, compatible with Linux/ROS.
  • Far-field voice pickup up to 5m, 360° pattern.
  • Acoustic algorithms: DOA, AEC, AGC, NS.
  • Publishes filtered audio to /audio/channel0 in ROS.
  • Supports VOSK and Google Cloud backends (VOSK preferred).
  • Can be tuned via ROS for noise suppression.

!speculation: Noise suppression can be adjusted via ROS; may improve performance in crowds.

items.title: Not detected in ROS
items.text:
• Check USB connection and device recognition.
  • Verify ROS node is running and publishing topics.

items.title: Poor audio quality
items.text:
• Adjust noise suppression settings.
  • Test device directly via USB for diagnostics.

items.title: Overview
items.text: This document summarizes the network ports used by the ARI robot and provides troubleshooting tips for common networking issues, including those encountered in virtual machine (VM) or simulator setups.

items.title: Port List
items.text: Port Protocol Usage/Service Notes 22 TCP SSH Remote shell access 53 TCP DNS Domain name resolution 80 TCP HTTP Web UI & REST interface 443 TCP HTTPS Apache server 3000 TCP HTTP Grafana monitoring 8080 TCP HTTP Webcommander 9090 TCP WebSocket ROS communication bridge 11011 TCP HTTP Presentation screen 8000 TCP HTTP-ALT Alternate HTTP 8001 TCP VCOM-Tunnel Knowledge base 8888 TCP Sun Answerbook 2003 TCP Finger 2004 TCP Mailbox 2809 TCP CORBA 6969 TCP ACMSoda

items.title: Example Network Traffic
items.text:
• Port 80: Used for arms control
  • Port 9090: Used for move base

items.title: Duplicate Routes in VM/Simulator
items.text:
If you experience asymmetric connectivity (e.g., robot can ping laptop but not vice versa), check for duplicate routes:

  • Remove conflicting route on eth1: 
    sudo ip route del 192.168.0.0/24 dev eth1
  • Ensure only one active route for the 192.168.0.0/24 network.

items.title: General Tips
items.text:
• Check physical connections and network cables.
  • Verify correct IP addresses and subnet masks.
  • Use ifconfig or ip addr to inspect network interfaces.
  • Use ping and traceroute for connectivity testing.

items.title: modifications
items.text:
to gain more control over the internal networking of the robot system we chose to install our own router in-place of a networking switch that came pre-installed. previously the ari-20c main mother-board acted as the router and dhcp server, with our router now in place we have disabled the dhcp daemon running on ari-20c the following is the steps taken to disable the "dhcp daemon"

pal@ari-20c:~$ su
pal@ari-20c:~$ rw
pal@ari-20c:~$ chroot /ro
pal@ari-20c:~$ systemctl disable isc-dhcp-server.service
pal@ari-20c:~$ systemctl mask isc-dhcp-server.service
pal@ari-20c:~$ exit
pal@ari-20c:~$ ro
pal@ari-20c:~$ exit
pal@ari-20c:~$ sudo reboot

items.title: Overview
items.text: This document explains how to use Docker containers with the ARI robot, including setup steps and troubleshooting common ROS networking issues.

items.title: Common Issue: Container Can't Resolve Hostname
items.text:
This is a common problem with ROS networking in Docker. To fix:

  1. Check if the container can ping itself by hostname: 
    ping $(hostname)
    If this fails, add the hostname to /etc/hosts: 
    echo "127.0.0.1 $(hostname)" | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts
  2. Set the ROS_HOSTNAME environment variable: 
    export ROS_HOSTNAME=localhost
  3. Try running roscore again: 
    roscore &
  4. Verify ROS is working: 
    rostopic list
    You should see at least the /rosout topic.

items.title: Using Explicit IP Addresses
items.text:
If issues persist, configure ROS with explicit IPs:

unset ROS_HOSTNAME
unset ROS_MASTER_URI
export ROS_IP=127.0.0.1
export ROS_MASTER_URI=http://127.0.0.1:11311
roscore &

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
• Ensure Docker containers have network access.
  • Check environment variables for correct ROS networking setup.

items.title: LLM Server
items.text: info. Description stock component No contains speculation Yes Location. External server or Jetson/Orin hardware related artical PCPartPicker Example

items.title: Description
items.text: The LLM Server is used to host large language models for ARI, providing advanced conversational and reasoning capabilities. Hardware options vary based on required model size and performance.

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Xavier NX: ~GTX 1050 performance, 0.5-1B parameter models.
  • Orin Nano: ~GTX 1650 performance.
  • TX2: GT 1030/MX150, <500M parameter models.
  • RTX 4070 SUPER: 7B-13B parameter models.
  • AGX Orin: 1-2B parameter models.
  • Jetson Nano: <100M parameter models.

!speculation: TX2 is the suggested upgrade for better performance.

items.title: Model runs slowly
items.text:
• Use more powerful hardware (see table above).
  • Optimize model size and quantization.

items.title: Server not detected
items.text:
• Check network connectivity and server status.
  • Verify correct model and dependencies are installed.

items.title: Localizer
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation Yes Location. /opt/pal/gallium/share/ari_2dnav (localizer ROS package) related artical PAL Application Management, ARI Navigation Config

items.title: Description
items.text: The localizer is responsible for ARI's self-localization using sensor data (e.g., cameras, LIDAR) and ROS packages. It is started by the pal_startup_manager and is critical for navigation and mapping.

items.title: Details
items.text:
the localizer nodes is a wrapper or modification of https://github.com/raulmur/ORB_SLAM2

  • Uses /opt/pal/gallium/share/ari_2dnav in an unknown capacity
  • !speculation: possibly related to pal_navigation_sm and pal_map_manager ros pakages
  • Startup managed by pal_startup_manager and pal_startup_node.
  • Relies on calibration data from /etc/calibration_data/ and ROS parameters.
  • May require specific calibration files (e.g., device_info.yaml).
  • Integrates with camera calibration and extrinsics (URDF/Xacro files).

!speculation: Some calibration files may be missing or incomplete on VMs; check real robot for full data.

items.title: Localization not starting
items.text:
• Check for missing calibration files in /etc/calibration_data/.
  • Verify pal_startup_manager and related ROS nodes are running.
  • Review logs for missing environment variables or parameters.

items.title: Poor localization accuracy
items.text:
• Ensure all sensors are calibrated.
  • Check for correct extrinsics and URDF files.

items.title: Overview
items.text:
This document summarizes the key ROS nodes, topics, and services used by the ARI robot, with examples and troubleshooting tips.

note: ros messages are in raw data and ros usually presents them to users as yaml, but our robot has an optional interface for access topics by standerd http (post and get) so that it can interact with the web based touch screen it exposes the data as json as seem below

items.title: Example: Publishing to a Topic
items.text:
To publish a message to a topic (e.g., /chatter):

{
  "op": "publish",
  "topic": "/chatter",
  "msg": {
    "data": "Hello via HTTP!"
  }
}

items.title: Useful Links
items.text: • PAL Robotics ARI SDK Topics

items.title: WebSocket Example
items.text:
Connect to the robot's ROS bridge:

wscat -c ws://192.168.0.100:9090

Example service calls: - List topics: { "op": "call_service", "service": "/rosapi/topics", "type": "rosapi/Topics" } - List services: { "op": "call_service", "service": "/rosapi/services", "type": "rosapi/Services" }

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
• Ensure all required ROS nodes are running.
  • Use rostopic list and rosservice list to inspect available topics and services.
  • Check logs for errors if communication fails.

items.title: Social Perception
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation No Location. See ARI documentation related artical PAL Social Perception Docs

items.title: Description
items.text: Social perception enables the ARI robot to detect, track, and interact with humans using vision and audio cues. It is a key part of human-robot interaction (HRI).

items.title: Details
items.text:
• Detects faces, bodies, and voices.
  • Tracks multiple people in real time.
  • Integrates with ARI's navigation and interaction systems.

related ros nodes inclued but may not be limeted to: - /hri_face_detect - /hri_face_identification - /hri_fullbody - /hri_overlay_throttle - /hri_person_manager - /hri_visualization

items.title: Social perception not working
items.text:
• Check camera and microphone functionality.
  • Ensure relevant ROS nodes are running.

items.title: tf
items.text: info. Description stock component Yes contains speculation Yes Location. ?? related artical ros wiki tf understanding ros transforms foxglove

items.title: Description
items.text:
the ari robot system uses the stock ros1 tf system for publishing and solving of all moving parts including its physical location relative to the world and the location of it arms and legs tf uses a one way tree node graph to track the positions and update the positions of components by "one way tree node graph" we mean each component has a parent child relation where a child node will never have a parent node higher up the tree be its child

this tree is encapsulated entirely with in the /tf ros topic so don’t confuse any ros topics with the same name as being the topic witch publishes its location

the components/nodes in the tf tree are called "frames" they represent each components of the robot and other objects in its environment(even non-moving part like a fixed camera ). frames are connected by transformations (position and rotation) relative to another frame as long as there are a chain of transformations between any 2 frames tf will be able to give you back the overall transformation between those 2 frames e.g

if there is a transformation from the world to the base and a transformations from the base to a hand then tf can give you the transformations from the world to the hand this is like a reverse version of how the unity game engine’s game object tree works

!speculation: the top frame in our case is the "/map" or "/world" frame representing its actual real world and ground, it is published by some node relating to the pal_navigation_sm and pal_map_manager ros pagkges

the frame representing the robots relative to the world is either "base_link" or "base_footprint"

the way that way that the transformations between frames are updated are

items.title: Details
items.text: !speculation: - Uses /opt/pal/gallium/share/ari_2dnav publish and or reads /tf
